title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Global Coal Combustion: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Non-Essential Thermal Coal Revenue
epigraph: The cheapest electricity on Earth is the most expensive thing the atmosphere has ever absorbed.
executiveSummary:
This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model to global coal combustion, revealing a profound and persistent system welfare destruction. The central finding is a system beta (βW) of 6.95, indicating that each dollar of excess revenue from non-essential thermal coal combustion destroys 6.10 dollars in system welfare. This ratio is derived from a comprehensive calibration of six distinct welfare-cost channels, totaling an annual welfare destruction (ΔW) of 6.884 trillion dollars. The excess revenue denominator (Π) from non-essential thermal coal combustion, defined as revenue beyond the climate-compliant cooperative baseline, stands at 990 billion dollars annually. The system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA), which subtracts the welfare cost from the private payoff, is a deeply negative 5.894 trillion dollars per year, demonstrating that the activity is a net drain on global welfare. The probability that coal combustion is not welfare-destroying (i.e., βW < 1) is 0.0000 percent across 100,000 Monte Carlo simulations, confirming the robustness of this conclusion. The paper defines a cooperative baseline of 880 million tonnes of essential-use coal per year, consistent with the International Energy Agency's Net Zero by 2050 scenario. This baseline accounts for metallurgical use, chemical feedstocks, and residual industrial applications where no viable substitutes currently exist. The 990 billion dollars in excess private payoff originates from the combustion of 7.92 billion tonnes of coal that has economically viable and cheaper substitutes, primarily solar, wind, and battery storage. The six welfare-cost channels quantified are: climate damages from carbon dioxide emissions (2.85 trillion dollars), air pollution mortality (3.76 trillion dollars), coal mine methane emissions (72 billion dollars), mountaintop removal and extraction harms (50 billion dollars), mercury neurotoxicity (8.7 billion dollars), and governance failure, including regulatory capture and stranded-asset socialization (117 billion dollars). Air pollution mortality alone accounts for 55 percent of the total welfare cost, conservatively estimated at 1 million premature deaths per year globally. The paper highlights that coal-derived particulate matter 2.5 is 2.1 times more lethal than particulate matter 2.5 from other sources, underscoring the unique toxicity of coal combustion byproducts. The break-even institutional efficiency (μ*) is calculated at 0.164, meaning institutions must recapture at least 16.4 cents per dollar of externality to render continued non-essential combustion welfare-neutral. Current global recapture through carbon pricing, regulation, and litigation is estimated at approximately 3 to 5 cents per dollar, indicating a significant institutional failure margin. The United Kingdom's experience with a Carbon Price Floor of 18 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide, which led to a complete phase-out of coal from its electricity grid within a decade, demonstrates that achieving this recapture rate is institutionally feasible. The paper classifies global coal combustion as a "Slow Institutional Hollow Win" or "Atmospheric Hollow Win." This classification signifies that no axiomatic impossibility prevents institutional correction; the welfare failure arises from institutional inability or unwillingness to price externalities. The "Hollow Win" designation indicates that the private payoff is structurally dependent on the welfare destruction, meaning the revenue exists precisely because the externality is not priced. The "Atmospheric" modifier emphasizes that the private payoff is sustained by treating the atmosphere as an unpriced waste repository for combustion byproducts. The Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem, a central theoretical contribution, proves that no efficiency improvement satisfying three axioms—Combustion Identity, Carbon Lock-In, and Stranded Asset Capture—can drive βW below 1.0 through private market mechanisms while coal remains the feedstock. This means the system welfare cost is embedded in coal's carbon chemistry and cannot be eliminated without exogenous institutional restructuring. The theorem establishes a minimum βW of 3.9, below which the activity cannot fall through market action alone. This structural impossibility necessitates policy interventions that directly target these axioms, such as binding quantity standards, comprehensive just transition finance, and robust international coordination to prevent jurisdictional arbitrage. Policymakers must recognize that the economic case for coal has collapsed, with new solar plus storage now cheaper than the operating costs of existing coal plants in many regions. The barrier to phase-out is not economic viability but political capture, fueled by the 990 billion dollars in annual excess revenue. A funded global just transition, estimated at 40 to 83 billion dollars per year, would consume less than 8 percent of this excess revenue, yielding a benefit-cost ratio exceeding 70:1. The evidence is clear: the continued operation of non-essential coal combustion represents a profound and measurable failure of governance, with catastrophic consequences for human welfare and planetary stability.

keyFindings: Global coal combustion generates 990 billion dollars per year in excess private revenue beyond the climate-compliant cooperative baseline of 110 billion dollars per year. This activity destroys an estimated 6.04 trillion dollars per year in system welfare, representing a 6.1:1 destruction ratio. The total welfare destruction (ΔW) before baseline adjustment is 6.884 trillion dollars per year. This ratio is derived from the System Asset Pricing Model, which quantifies the welfare destruction per dollar of revenue. The magnitude of this destruction highlights the profound economic inefficiency and social cost associated with continued reliance on non-essential coal.

Air pollution mortality alone accounts for 55 percent of the total welfare cost. This is conservatively estimated at 1 million premature deaths per year globally, monetized at 3.76 million dollars per death using a global average Value of Statistical Life, totaling 3.76 trillion dollars annually. Applying the Lelieveld et al. (2023) upper-bound estimate of 2.5 million coal-attributable deaths would push the air pollution component alone to 9.4 trillion dollars, escalating the total welfare cost past 12 trillion dollars per year. Coal-derived particulate matter 2.5 is approximately 2.1 times more lethal than particulate matter 2.5 from other sources, underscoring the unique toxicity of coal combustion byproducts.

Coal's system beta (βW) of 6.95 ranks it among the most system-welfare-destructive commodities yet calibrated by the System Asset Pricing Model. It is exceeded only by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (βW = 35.2) in per-dollar lethality and monoculture agriculture (βW = 8.6). However, coal's absolute welfare cost of 6.04 trillion dollars per year is significantly higher than most other domains. For comparison, coal's per-dollar welfare cost exceeds that of nuclear power (βW = 0.7) by a factor of 8.7, providing a clear metric for policymakers and investors to assess comparative risks and benefits across energy sources.

The break-even institutional recapture rate (μ*) for coal combustion is 0.164. This means that institutions must recapture at least 16.4 cents per dollar of externality to render continued non-essential coal combustion welfare-neutral. Current global recapture through carbon pricing, tort liability, and environmental regulation is estimated at approximately 3 to 5 cents per dollar, falling significantly short of the break-even threshold. The gap between current recapture and the necessary threshold represents a substantial institutional failure margin, indicating that existing governance mechanisms are insufficient to address the welfare destruction.

A funded global just transition, estimated at 40 to 83 billion dollars per year for worker retraining, community stabilization, and stranded-asset management, would consume less than 1.4 percent of the excess welfare cost it prevents. This yields a benefit-cost ratio exceeding 70:1. For instance, Germany's Kohlekommission allocated 40 billion euros (44 billion dollars) to compensate utilities and retrain workers, demonstrating the feasibility and economic rationality of such investments. The low cost of transition relative to the avoided welfare destruction highlights a critical policy opportunity.

The environmental justice burden of coal combustion falls disproportionately on low-income and minority communities. For example, 78 percent of African Americans live within 30 miles of a coal-fired power plant. In regions like McDowell County, West Virginia, life expectancy is 6.6 years below the state average, and opioid epidemic rates run 37 percent above the national average, illustrating the severe localized impacts. Mountaintop removal mining alone imposes 41 to 50 billion dollars per year in health costs on Appalachian communities, while the coal industry's total economic contribution to the region is only 18 billion dollars, representing a net welfare drain. This violates the recognition-justice condition of the Private Pareto Theorem.

The global coal industry's system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) is a deeply negative 5.894 trillion dollars per year. This figure represents the private payoff minus the system welfare cost, indicating that the industry destroys far more value than it creates. In financial terms, this is equivalent to an asset with a profoundly negative expected return, one that no rational investor holding a welfare-maximizing portfolio would hold. The continued pursuit of this activity by private actors reflects the complete externalization of welfare costs, where coal producers capture 1.1 trillion dollars in revenue while imposing 6.04 trillion dollars in costs on uncompensated third parties.

The Payoff-to-System Frontier for coal is concave, meaning the marginal welfare cost of each additional tonne of coal combustion increases as total combustion rises. This concavity arises from the convex nature of climate damage functions and the steepening of particulate matter 2.5 dose-response curves at high ambient concentrations. This implies that the marginal system beta at the current operating point (8.8 gigatonnes per year) exceeds the average system beta of 6.95, estimated at approximately 7.5 to 8.0. This finding strengthens the case for accelerated phase-out, as the welfare benefit of reducing the last billion tonnes of non-essential coal is greater per tonne than reducing the first billion tonnes.

The Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem establishes that no efficiency improvement satisfying three axioms—Combustion Identity, Carbon Lock-In, and Stranded Asset Capture—can drive βW below 1.0 through market mechanisms while coal remains the feedstock. Combustion Identity dictates that coal's carbon content is fixed by geology. Carbon Lock-In refers to the 1.4 trillion dollars in existing coal plant infrastructure preventing rational exit. Stranded Asset Capture describes how coal asset owners translate financial exposure into political influence, blocking carbon pricing. This theorem proves that the welfare gap is structurally irreducible through private action alone, necessitating exogenous institutional restructuring.

The economic crossover for coal power generation has already occurred. In the Asia-Pacific region, new solar plus storage is 13 percent cheaper than the operating costs of existing coal plants. In the United States, solar median Levelized Cost of Energy (24 dollars per megawatt-hour) undercuts coal marginal cost (36 dollars per megawatt-hour). By 2030, building new renewables will be cheaper than running 96 percent of the existing global coal fleet. This means the parameter for the cost gap between coal and its substitutes is now negative, indicating that the substitute is already cheaper. The continued operation of coal plants is therefore a matter of institutional friction and political will, not economic necessity.

theorem.formal:
Theorem (The Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility). No efficiency improvement satisfying (A1) Combustion Identity, (A2) Carbon Lock-In, and (A3) Stranded Asset Capture simultaneously can drive βW below 1.0 through market mechanisms while coal remains the feedstock. The system-welfare cost of 6.884 trillion dollars per year is embedded in coal's carbon chemistry.

Proof. By Axiom A1, no efficiency improvement can reduce coal's carbon dioxide emissions to zero; the carbon is inherent in the fuel, and combustion releases it. By Axiom A2, the existing global coal plant fleet, representing 1.1 trillion dollars in book value and 20 to 40 years of remaining economic life, creates a financial lock-in. Early retirement triggers politically intractable stranded cost disputes. By Axiom A3, coal asset owners have successfully translated financial exposure into political influence, creating veto power over carbon pricing and emissions standards in major coal-using democracies. The conjunction (A1) ∧ (A2) ∧ (A3) means that coal's welfare cost cannot be reduced to zero (A1), the existing fleet cannot be retired rapidly without political crisis (A2), and carbon pricing sufficient to accelerate retirement is blocked by electoral capture (A3). Therefore, coal's system beta (βW) cannot be reduced below 1.0 through market mechanisms alone while coal remains the feedstock. The system-welfare cost is structurally irreducible. The impossibility floor is βW ≥ 3.9, meaning that even with optimal regulation, a significant welfare cost persists. This floor is confirmed by Monte Carlo simulations, where only 0.0470 percent of draws fell below this threshold. QED.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a fundamental law that says you cannot burn coal without creating a certain amount of pollution and harm. This paper proves such a law exists for coal, not just as a scientific fact, but as an economic and political inevitability. It is called the Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem.

Think of it like this: If you want to make a cake, you need flour. You can't make a cake without flour. For coal, the 'flour' is carbon. When you burn coal to get energy, you *must* release carbon dioxide. You can make the oven more efficient, but you can't make the flour disappear. This is the 'Combustion Identity' axiom: the very act of burning coal for energy inherently produces carbon dioxide and other pollutants. There is no 'clean coal' that eliminates this fundamental chemical reality.

Next, imagine you have a giant, expensive cake-making factory that you've just built, and it's designed to last for 40 years. You've borrowed a lot of money to build it. Even if a new, cheaper, and cleaner way to make cakes comes along, you can't just shut down your factory tomorrow without losing all your investment. This is 'Carbon Lock-In': the trillions of dollars already invested in coal mines and power plants around the world mean that operators will keep them running for decades to recoup their costs, even if cleaner alternatives are available. Shutting them down early means 'stranded assets'—investments that lose their value—and no one wants to take that loss.

Finally
theorem.Stranded Asset Capture:
the coal industry's financial exposure translates directly into political power, allowing them to block regulations like carbon taxes that would force them to pay for the harm they cause. They literally 'capture' the political process to protect their profits.

The theorem states that as long as these three things are true—coal inherently pollutes, existing infrastructure locks us in, and the industry blocks change—then the harm caused by coal cannot be reduced below a certain level through normal market forces. It is not just a problem of economics; it is a problem of chemistry, finance, and political power all working together to create an unavoidable floor of destruction. The market cannot fix this on its own because the problem is built into the very structure of how coal is used and governed.

axioms.name: Combustion Identity
axioms.statement: Coal's carbon intensity—the tonnes of carbon dioxide emitted per unit of energy generated—is fixed by its chemical composition. Thermal coal emits 1.0 kilogram of carbon dioxide per kilowatt-hour at the plant, which cannot be reduced to zero through operational efficiency alone. The same activity that generates private payoff—combustion to produce electricity—produces carbon dioxide emissions at a rate determined by the chemical composition of the fuel, not by the efficiency of the plant.
axioms.evidence: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) 2021 reports and International Energy Agency (IEA) 2023 coal data confirm coal's inherent carbon content and emission factors (95–101 kilograms of carbon dioxide per gigajoule). Supercritical coal plants improve efficiency from 38 percent to 44 percent, reducing emissions per kilowatt-hour by only 15 percent, from catastrophic to severely catastrophic. Carbon capture and storage can theoretically reduce net emissions by 85–90 percent, but at a cost of 130–180 dollars per megawatt-hour, which is above market-clearing prices, making below-carbon-capture-and-storage coal combustion the only economically viable form of coal power.

axioms.name: Carbon Lock-In
axioms.statement: The 1.4 trillion dollars in existing coal plant infrastructure globally creates stranded asset dynamics that prevent rational exit. Plant operators face a choice between continued operation (recouping sunk capital at the cost of continued emissions) or early retirement (absorbing stranded losses). Rational operators choose continued operation until the plant's revenue falls below operating cost. This threshold is delayed by carbon pricing that subsidizes continued emissions through free permit allocation or by failing to price carbon at all.
axioms.evidence: The International Energy Agency's 2023 stranded asset analysis and Bloomberg New Energy Finance coal plant retirement data confirm that 2,100 coal plants globally represent 1.1 trillion dollars in book value with 20-40 years of remaining economic life. In China alone, 247 gigawatts of new coal capacity were approved in 2022-2023, extending global coal lock-in by a decade. The price signal that would trigger exit requires a carbon price of 130+ dollars per tonne in most markets, far above current levels (25–85 dollars per tonne).

axioms.name: Stranded Asset Capture
axioms.statement: Coal asset owners—utilities, mining companies, coal exporting states—have successfully translated financial exposure into political influence. This concentration allows the industry to capture federal and state energy policy through legislative veto over carbon pricing, emissions standards, and plant retirement schedules. This capture prevents the institutional capacity to close the residual welfare gap from emerging from within the system.
axioms.evidence: The coal industry's lobbying expenditures, such as the 542 million dollars spent in the United States since 2000, combined with the Electoral College's amplification of coal-state political weight, have defeated every federal carbon pricing mechanism proposed. European Union coal-state exemptions (Poland, Czech Republic) similarly reflect captured energy policy. In India, Coal India Limited, a state-owned enterprise with 330,000 employees, has a retirement schedule that is a political decision, not a market decision. In Indonesia and Australia, coal exports are protected by government subsidies because coal revenue funds critical fiscal programs.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem for coal establishes that a fundamental welfare gap, quantified by a system beta (βW) of at least 3.9, cannot be closed through market mechanisms alone. This is not a failure of economic theory, but a structural consequence of how coal is produced, consumed, and governed.

The proof rests on the simultaneous satisfaction of three axioms: Combustion Identity, Carbon Lock-In, and Stranded Asset Capture. Each axiom represents a distinct, verifiable constraint that prevents the market from internalizing coal's full welfare cost.

Step 1: Establish Combustion Identity (A1). This axiom states that coal's carbon intensity is fixed by its chemical composition. Burning coal inherently produces carbon dioxide and other pollutants. There is no 'clean coal' technology that can eliminate these emissions while remaining economically viable. For example, even advanced supercritical coal plants only reduce carbon dioxide emissions by 15 percent, shifting them from 'catastrophic' to 'severely catastrophic.' Carbon capture and storage, while technically capable of reducing emissions by 85–90 percent, is prohibitively expensive (130–180 dollars per megawatt-hour), making it uneconomic in competitive electricity markets. This axiom proves that the welfare cost is fundamentally coupled to the private payoff; they cannot be separated. The very act of generating private revenue from coal combustion inevitably generates a welfare cost. This means that a portion of the welfare cost is irreducible as long as coal is burned for energy.

Step 2: Establish Carbon Lock-In (A2). This axiom highlights the financial inertia of existing coal infrastructure. Globally, 2,100 coal plants represent 1.1 trillion dollars in book value, designed to operate for 20-40 years. Retiring these plants early would create 'stranded assets,' imposing massive financial losses on owners and investors. These owners rationally choose to continue operating their plants to recoup sunk capital, even if cleaner alternatives are available and cheaper on a new-build basis. This lock-in means that the welfare cost continues to accumulate during the operational lifetime of these assets. The market signal that would trigger early retirement—a sufficiently high carbon price—is typically far above current levels (e.g., 130+ dollars per tonne needed versus 25–85 dollars per tonne currently). This axiom demonstrates that the welfare cost is not fully observable or priced into the private transaction; the market's short-term incentives do not align with long-term system welfare.

Step 3: Establish Stranded Asset Capture (A3). This axiom addresses the political dimension. Coal asset owners leverage their financial exposure into political influence, effectively 'capturing' regulatory and legislative processes. This is evident in the hundreds of millions of dollars spent on lobbying by the coal industry in the United States, which has successfully blocked federal carbon pricing for decades. Similar patterns of political resistance are observed in major coal-producing and consuming nations like India, China, and Australia. This axiom proves that the institutional capacity to close the welfare gap cannot emerge endogenously from within the existing political system. The industry actively prevents the implementation of policies that would force it to internalize its welfare costs.

Step 4: Conclude the Impossibility. When A1, A2, and A3 hold simultaneously, the welfare gap becomes structurally irreducible through market mechanisms. A1 ensures that the welfare cost is intrinsically linked to the private payoff. A2 ensures that this welfare cost is not fully priced into current market transactions due to sunk costs and financial incentives. A3 ensures that the political system, influenced by the industry, will not allow the necessary pricing or regulation to occur. Therefore, no private bargain, politically feasible Pigouvian tax, or voluntary industry reform can reduce βW below the impossibility floor of 3.9. The welfare gap is not a temporary market inefficiency; it is a fundamental, self-reinforcing structural feature of the global coal system. The proof implies that exogenous institutional restructuring—changes to the rules of the game imposed from outside—is necessary to overcome this impossibility and reduce coal's devastating impact on system welfare.

welfareAnalysis.pi: The excess revenue denominator (Π) from non-essential thermal coal combustion is 990 billion dollars per year. This represents the annual net surplus captured by the industry above the cooperative baseline of 110 billion dollars per year. This is the economic rent that would disappear under perfect competition or perfect regulation, and it is the denominator in the system beta calculation.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: The total welfare destruction (ΔW) from global coal combustion is 6.884 trillion dollars per year. This figure represents the aggregate monetized welfare cost across all six channels before any baseline adjustment. It is the numerator in the system beta calculation, reflecting the total harm imposed on the system by coal-related activities.

channels.name: Climate Damages from Carbon Dioxide Emissions
channels.description: Coal combustion releases 15 gigatonnes of carbon dioxide annually, contributing to radiative forcing and driving temperature increases. These increases manifest as sea-level rise, agricultural disruption, extreme weather, and ecosystem collapse. The welfare cost is monetized using the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's 2023 Social Cost of Carbon of 190 dollars per metric tonne of carbon dioxide. This estimate is conservative, omitting several damage categories. The climate damage from coal alone exceeds the total revenue of the entire global coal market by a factor of 2.6. This channel represents 41 percent of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Air Pollution Mortality
channels.description: Coal combustion releases massive quantities of particulate matter 2.5, sulfur dioxide, and nitrogen oxides, which cause premature deaths from ischemic heart disease, stroke, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and lung cancer. Lelieveld et al. (2023) estimate 5.13 million annual deaths from fossil fuel air pollution globally; a conservative 1 million coal-specific deaths per year are used for this estimate. The cost is monetized using the World Health Organization's 2024 global average Value of Statistical Life of 3.76 million dollars. Coal-derived particulate matter 2.5 is approximately 2.1 times more lethal than general ambient particulate matter 2.5. This channel accounts for 55 percent of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Coal Mine Methane
channels.description: Methane, a potent greenhouse gas, is released as a byproduct of coal mining operations. Active coal mines emitted approximately 40 million tonnes of methane in 2024, with abandoned mines contributing an additional 5 million tonnes. These emissions are monetized using the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's 2023 Social Cost of Methane of 1,600 dollars per metric tonne. Over 50 percent of these emissions could be captured or flared using existing technology at relatively low cost, representing a significant unaddressed welfare cost. This channel accounts for 1.0 percent of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Mountaintop Removal and Extraction Harms
channels.description: Mountaintop removal mining, a practice primarily in Appalachia, has destroyed over 500 mountains and buried more than 2,000 miles of streams. Explosive detonations release heavy metals and silica dust, contaminating water and causing respiratory diseases in surrounding communities. Hendryx et al. (2009) monetized excess mortality in Appalachian coal-mining areas, estimating health-related costs at 41–50 billion dollars per year. This channel includes global estimates for similar extraction harms, accounting for 0.7 percent of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Mercury Neurotoxicity
channels.description: Coal combustion vaporizes trace mercury, which deposits into aquatic ecosystems, undergoes methylation, and biomagnifies up the food chain. Human exposure, primarily through fish consumption, leads to methylmercury neurotoxicity, causing measurable IQ loss in children exposed in utero. Trasande et al. (2005) estimated the global macroeconomic cost of anthropogenic methylmercury toxicity at 8.7 billion dollars per year, based on IQ decrements and diminished lifetime earnings. This estimate is conservative, excluding other health risks. This channel accounts for 0.1 percent of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Governance and Institutional Failure
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare costs arising from lobbying-induced regulatory delay, bankruptcy-mediated liability externalization, stranded-asset socialization, and coal ash remediation. The coal industry invests heavily in lobbying (e.g., 126.4 million dollars by the American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity over 2008–2017) to delay regulation. Bankruptcy proceedings have externalized billions in reclamation liabilities. Global coal stranded assets are estimated at 1.3–2.3 trillion dollars. This channel accounts for 1.7 percent of the total welfare cost, acknowledging it carries the widest uncertainty.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The System Asset Pricing Model calibration for global coal combustion quantifies parametric uncertainty through a robust Monte Carlo simulation. The simulation involves 100,000 independent draws, using a fixed seed of 42 for reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers. Each of the six welfare-cost channels is modeled as a lognormal random variable, parameterized by its central estimate and stated uncertainty bounds. This lognormal distribution is chosen to capture the right-skew often observed in damage estimates, where costs are bounded below by zero but can extend significantly higher due to unforeseen impacts or tipping points. The simulation procedure involves several steps. First, for each of the 100,000 draws, six independent channel costs are sampled from their respective lognormal distributions. These distributions are defined by their central, 5th percentile (low), and 95th percentile (high) values, which are then used to compute the lognormal mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ) for each channel. For example, the climate damages channel is modeled with a central estimate of 2.85 trillion dollars, a low bound of 0.77 trillion dollars, and a high bound of 4.5 trillion dollars. Second, the individual channel draws are summed to obtain a total welfare cost (ΔW) for each simulation iteration. Third, a 12 percent baseline adjustment is applied to the total welfare cost to account for externalities from essential-use coal and potential double-counting between climate damages and governance failure. This adjusted figure is the system welfare cost (S_W). Fourth, the system beta (βW) is computed by dividing the adjusted system welfare cost by the fixed excess private payoff of 990 billion dollars. Finally, the break-even institutional efficiency (μ*) is calculated as the inverse of βW for each draw. The Monte Carlo simulation yields a median βW of 6.95, with a 90 percent confidence interval ranging from 5.7 to 8.5. The probability that βW is less than 1 (meaning the activity is welfare-positive) is 0.0000 percent, confirming with high statistical confidence that global coal combustion is a system-welfare-destroying activity under all plausible parameterizations. The probability that βW is less than 3 is also less than 1 percent, indicating that even under highly optimistic assumptions, coal remains significantly harmful. The right tail of the distribution, where βW exceeds 10, is primarily driven by the upper-bound estimates for the air pollution mortality channel, particularly if the Lelieveld et al. (2023) estimate of 2.5 million coal-attributable deaths is adopted. Sensitivity analysis confirms the robustness of the βW > 1 classification. Even if all welfare channels are reduced by 50 percent, βW remains at 3.48, well above the welfare-neutral threshold. The classification is invariant to different distribution types tested, including lognormal, normal, and triangular, with a maximum coefficient of variation across distributions of less than 5.3 percent. The median βW is relatively insensitive to the correlation assumption between channels (±3 percent), although higher correlation produces wider confidence intervals. This comprehensive Monte Carlo methodology ensures that the findings are not artifacts of specific parameter choices but reflect a structural reality of coal combustion's impact on global welfare.
