caseStudies.title: United Kingdom Carbon Price Floor (2013–2024)
caseStudies.content: The United Kingdom's Carbon Price Floor represents a decisive and successful game transformation for coal phase-out. Implemented in April 2013, the Carbon Price Support rate set a minimum carbon cost of 16 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide, layered atop the European Union Emissions Trading System. This national fiscal instrument, enacted through the Finance Act 2011, did not require international agreement or supranational enforcement. The Carbon Price Floor made coal-fired generation uneconomic relative to gas-fired generation within 18 months. By April 2015, the rate escalated to 18 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide, achieving its target. As a direct result, coal's share of United Kingdom electricity generation plummeted from 40 percent in 2012 to 0 percent by September 2024. This complete phase-out was achieved in approximately 11 years. The estimated institutional recapture rate (μ) for United Kingdom-specific coal externalities was approximately 0.95, the highest of any documented jurisdiction. The annual cost to consumers peaked at approximately 36 pounds per household. This transformation demonstrates that the break-even institutional recapture threshold of 0.164 is achievable when political will aligns with economic viability. The Carbon Price Floor effectively removed coal's implicit atmospheric subsidy, proving that a modest carbon price can eliminate a major fossil fuel from a national grid.

caseStudies.title: United States Shale Gas Revolution (2008–2024)
caseStudies.content: The United States shale gas revolution, driven by hydraulic fracturing innovation, led to a market-driven fuel switching from coal to natural gas. This transformation reduced natural gas prices by approximately 69 percent since 2008. While primarily a market-based intervention (Tier 2), it was supported by federal regulatory actions such as the Environmental Protection Agency's Mercury and Air Toxics Standards and the Clean Air Act. As a result, United States coal generation fell dramatically from 50 percent in 2005 to 15 percent in 2024. Over 9,300 megawatts of coal capacity were scheduled for closure or conversion in 2025 alone. The institutional recapture rate (μ) achieved was approximately 0.15–0.20. This transformation partially internalized coal's cost disadvantage through market competition, but it did not fully price the carbon dioxide externality. Natural gas, while lower-carbon than coal, is not zero-carbon. The health benefits from reduced coal particulate matter 2.5 were substantial, with 460,000 avoided elderly deaths between 1999 and 2020. However, the absence of a comprehensive carbon price means that natural gas largely replaced coal instead of renewables, limiting the overall climate benefit. The United States is not on track for a 2030 Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development coal phase-out, with delayed retirements extending coal plant lifetimes due to data-center demand growth and grid reliability concerns.

caseStudies.title: Germany's Kohlekommission (2018–2038)
caseStudies.content: Germany's Kohlekommission (Coal Commission) established a legislated coal phase-out by 2038, backed by a 40 billion euro (44 billion dollar) package for worker compensation, regional economic restructuring, and utility buyouts. This federal legislative intervention (Tier 3) was heavily conditioned by state-level and labor-union consensus. Germany has retired substantial coal capacity, but the 2022 energy crisis forced the temporary reactivation of 8 gigawatts, and Eastern German states continue to resist acceleration. The institutional recapture rate (μ) achieved is approximately 0.40–0.50. The 40 billion euro package internalizes significant transition costs, with an annualized cost of approximately 2.2 billion euros (2.4 billion dollars) per year. For the 32,800 direct workers, this amounts to approximately 1.2 million euros per worker over the transition period, or 67,000 euros per year for 18 years. While Germany's consensus model is a global benchmark for just transition design, its 2038 timeline is misaligned with the 1.5 degrees Celsius pathways requiring Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development exit by 2030. This transformation demonstrates that institutional recapture is achievable through fiscal transfer but is constrained by the political economy of coal-dependent regions.

caseStudies.title: China Ultra-Low Emission Standards (2014–ongoing)
caseStudies.content: China's Ultra-Low Emission Standards represent a centralized state mandate (Tier 3) requiring coal plants to achieve gas-turbine-equivalent emission levels for localized pollutants. These standards mandated reductions to 35 milligrams per cubic meter for sulfur dioxide, 50 milligrams per cubic meter for nitrogen oxides, and 10 milligrams per cubic meter for particulate matter. As a result, China achieved a 65 percent reduction in sulfur dioxide, a 60 percent reduction in nitrogen oxides, and a 72 percent reduction in particulate matter from coal plants, ahead of its 2020 target. This is the most successful end-of-pipe pollution control program in history. However, during the same period, total coal consumption and carbon dioxide emissions continued to grow, with carbon dioxide emissions rising by 10 percent. The institutional recapture rate (μ) achieved was approximately 0.15 for localized pollutants but only 0.02 for carbon dioxide. This transformation illustrates the critical distinction between pollution mitigation and fuel displacement. China's Ultra-Low Emission standards were welfare-improving on the mortality channel by cleaning the air, but welfare-neutral on the climate channel because they did not reduce coal consumption or carbon dioxide emissions. This case study highlights the importance of the System Asset Pricing Model's multi-channel architecture in capturing the nuanced impacts of policy interventions.

caseStudies.title: South Africa Just Energy Transition Partnership (2021–ongoing)
caseStudies.content: The South Africa Just Energy Transition Partnership involved an 8.5 billion dollar (later 12.4 billion dollar) international financing pledge to decommission Eskom's coal fleet and build renewable capacity. This multilateral treaty (Tier 4) was to be executed via national executive action (Tier 3). However, it has achieved near-zero coal reduction to date, assessed by independent analysts as a 'stagnation/slow growth' partnership. The institutional recapture rate (μ) achieved is approximately 0.01. The partnership has disbursed minimal funds, with the vast majority (96 percent) structured as loans rather than grants. The United States' withdrawal in 2025, canceling 56 million dollars in grants and 1 billion dollars in Development Finance Corporation potential, further destabilized the initiative. Loading 8.2 billion dollars in additional sovereign debt onto a country with 70 percent debt-to-GDP does not internalize coal externalities; it transfers the cost of transition from coal producers to South African taxpayers while preserving the coal industry's private payoff. This demonstrates the structural inadequacy of debt-financed transition in developing economies. The μ* threshold of 0.164 cannot be met by creating new financial costs rather than internalizing existing ones; grant-heavy financing is required to achieve genuine welfare recapture.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework governing global coal combustion is fragmented, inconsistent, and largely insufficient to address the profound system welfare destruction documented in this paper. No single, globally harmonized framework exists, leading to significant jurisdictional arbitrage and enforcement gaps. Instead, a patchwork of national and regional regulations, international agreements, and voluntary initiatives attempts to mitigate coal's impacts. In the United States, the Environmental Protection Agency holds primary jurisdiction over air pollution from coal-fired power plants under the Clean Air Act. Key regulations include the Mercury and Air Toxics Standards, which significantly reduced mercury and particulate matter emissions, and Section 111 of the Clean Air Act, which historically set greenhouse gas emission standards for power plants. However, the effectiveness of Section 111 has been subject to political reversals, including the Trump administration's 2025 proposal to repeal greenhouse gas standards, arguing that power-plant greenhouse gases 'do not contribute significantly to dangerous air pollution'—a claim contradicted by the Environmental Protection Agency's own scientific findings. The Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement oversees coal mining reclamation, but loopholes like self-bonding have allowed companies to externalize liabilities. In the European Union, the Emissions Trading System is the cornerstone of carbon pricing, covering approximately 10 percent of global coal emissions. While the European Union Emissions Trading System price has fluctuated between 50 and 80 euros per tonne of carbon dioxide in 2024, it remains below the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Social Cost of Carbon of 190 dollars per tonne. The European Union's 'Fit for 55' package aims for deeper emission cuts, but member states like Poland and the Czech Republic have secured exemptions or extended timelines for coal phase-out. The Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, set to be fully implemented by 2026, aims to prevent carbon leakage by imposing tariffs on carbon-intensive imports. China, the world's largest coal consumer, has implemented Ultra-Low Emission Standards for coal-fired power plants, significantly reducing local air pollutants like sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and particulate matter. However, these standards did not address carbon dioxide emissions, which continued to grow. China's energy policy is centrally planned, with the National Energy Administration overseeing coal production and consumption. Despite massive renewable energy deployment, China continues to approve new coal capacity, often for 'flexibilization' to back up intermittent renewables. India, the second-largest coal consumer, lacks a comprehensive carbon pricing mechanism. The Ministry of Coal and Coal India Limited, a state-owned enterprise, dominate the sector. Environmental regulations exist but enforcement is often weak, leading to severe air pollution and environmental degradation. International treaties like the Paris Agreement provide a framework for national climate commitments, but these are largely voluntary and lack robust enforcement mechanisms. Initiatives like the Powering Past Coal Alliance, with 180 members, aim to accelerate coal phase-out but cover only a third of the world's governments and 69 percent of global operating coal capacity has no planned closure commitment before 2050.
Statute/Regulation Jurisdiction Primary Focus Effectiveness for Coal
Clean Air Act (§111, MATS) United States Air pollution, greenhouse gases Significant for local pollutants, inconsistent for greenhouse gases
Emissions Trading System European Union Carbon pricing Effective where price is high, limited by coverage and exemptions
Carbon Price Floor United Kingdom Carbon pricing Highly effective, led to complete coal phase-out
Ultra-Low Emission Standards China Local air pollution Highly effective for local pollutants, no impact on carbon dioxide
Paris Agreement International National climate commitments Voluntary, lacks strong enforcement mechanisms, insufficient for 1.5°C target
Mine Safety and Health Act United States Worker safety in mining Partial, significant undercounting of diseases like progressive massive fibrosis
policyAnalysis.failures:
The current regulatory and legal framework fails to address global coal combustion's profound welfare destruction for several structural reasons, primarily rooted in the axioms of the Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem: Combustion Identity, Carbon Lock-In, and Stranded Asset Capture.

First, the framework's failure to adequately price carbon dioxide externalities is a critical flaw. Pigouvian taxation, which theoretically internalizes marginal social damage, fails operationally. The tax must be applied globally, simultaneously, and at the correct level (e.g., 190 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide, per the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Social Cost of Carbon). This has never been achieved. Jurisdictional fragmentation means that while the United Kingdom successfully implemented a Carbon Price Floor, major coal consumers like China (51 percent of global output) and India (10 percent) have no economy-wide carbon tax. The European Union Emissions Trading System, while a step forward, prices carbon at roughly one-third of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's estimate, rendering it insufficient to force a fundamental fuel switch. This failure to price carbon allows the 'Atmospheric Subsidy' of 6.04 trillion dollars per year to persist, directly enabling the private payoff from welfare-destroying activities.

Second, regulatory capture and lobbying by the coal industry systematically weaken and delay effective regulation. The American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity spent 126.4 million dollars over 2008–2017 on 'clean coal' advertising, specifically designed to obscure the link between coal combustion and welfare harm. Peabody Energy, a major coal producer, funded climate-denial organizations while simultaneously filing for bankruptcy protection from environmental liabilities. This lobbying translates into legislative veto power, particularly in coal-dependent regions. For example, coal's outsized electoral importance in states like West Virginia has blocked federal carbon pricing for three decades in the United States. This directly fulfills the Stranded Asset Capture axiom, where financial exposure is translated into political influence to prevent welfare-correcting policies.

Third, enforcement gaps and jurisdictional arbitrage undermine the effectiveness of existing laws. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Section 111 greenhouse gas standards, designed to reduce emissions from power plants, have faced repeated legal challenges and proposed repeals, demonstrating the fragility of regulatory efforts. Loopholes like self-bonding in mining regulations allowed companies like Peabody, Alpha, and Arch to hold 2.4 billion dollars in reclamation liabilities without setting aside actual money, externalizing these costs onto taxpayers during bankruptcy. Internationally, the South Africa Just Energy Transition Partnership, intended to accelerate coal phase-out, largely failed due to a 96 percent loan-to-grant ratio, which transferred transition costs to the sovereign balance sheet rather than internalizing them for coal producers. This jurisdictional arbitrage allows coal production to shift to regions with weaker environmental standards, perpetuating the welfare destruction globally.

Fourth, the framework often addresses symptoms rather than root causes. China's Ultra-Low Emission Standards, while highly effective at reducing local air pollutants, did not reduce carbon dioxide emissions, which continued to grow. This 'channel-specific' success highlights a failure to address the fundamental Combustion Identity axiom: coal inherently produces carbon dioxide. The framework's inability to mandate a shift away from coal itself, even when economically viable substitutes exist, means that the core problem of carbon intensity remains unaddressed. The continued construction of new coal plants, such as the 78 gigawatts approved in China in 2025, further entrenches Carbon Lock-In, extending the problem for decades and accumulating more stranded asset risk.
policyAnalysis.reform:
Reforming the current framework to address coal's system welfare destruction requires a multi-pronged approach that directly targets the axioms of the Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem: Combustion Identity, Carbon Lock-In, and Stranded Asset Capture. The goal is to achieve an institutional recapture rate (μ) of at least 0.164, the break-even threshold for welfare neutrality.

First, implement a robust carbon price floor at μ*-equivalent levels. The United Kingdom's Carbon Price Floor, which set a minimum carbon cost of 18 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide, proved highly effective, leading to a complete coal phase-out in 11 years. This demonstrates that a national fiscal instrument can achieve the necessary externality internalization. A global carbon price floor of 30–50 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide, escalating annually toward the full U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Social Cost of Carbon of 190 dollars per tonne, would make coal-fired power uneconomic in most markets. To prevent jurisdictional arbitrage, Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanisms, like the European Union's, are crucial to protect early movers and incentivize global adoption. This directly addresses the Carbon Lock-In axiom by making continued operation financially untenable and the Stranded Asset Capture axiom by reducing the profitability that fuels political resistance.

Second, establish comprehensive and adequately funded Just Transition Finance mechanisms. The estimated cost of a global just transition for affected workers and communities is 40–83 billion dollars per year, less than 8 percent of the annual excess coal revenue. Germany's Kohlekommission, which allocated 40 billion euros (44 billion dollars) for worker compensation, retraining, and regional economic restructuring, provides a proven model. These funds must be grant-heavy, not debt-heavy, to avoid transferring costs to sovereign balance sheets, as seen in the failed South Africa Just Energy Transition Partnership. By addressing the distributional friction of phase-out, just transition finance reduces political opposition and weakens the Stranded Asset Capture axiom, making accelerated retirement politically feasible.

Third, implement robust stranded-asset securitization and managed early-retirement programs. The 1.3–2.3 trillion dollars in global coal stranded assets can be managed through structured mechanisms. The Asian Development Bank's Energy Transition Mechanism model, expanded Just Energy Transition Partnerships with grant-heavy financing, and coal-plant-to-renewable conversion funds are essential. The cancellation of the Cirebon-1 early retirement project in Indonesia, where the buyout cost was 8.4 billion dollars, highlights the inadequacy of current mechanisms. Reforms must cover the full power purchase agreement buyout cost, not just a fraction, to incentivize utilities to retire plants early. This directly confronts the Carbon Lock-In axiom by providing a financial pathway out of long-term commitments.

Fourth, enforce stringent emission performance standards that mandate fuel switching, not just pollution control. China's Ultra-Low Emission Standards, while effective for local air pollutants, failed to reduce carbon dioxide emissions. Future regulations must target carbon intensity directly, such as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's proposed 90 percent carbon capture and storage mandate (though now under legal challenge). This addresses the Combustion Identity axiom by forcing a fundamental change in the energy source or its emissions profile. The United Kingdom's success in phasing out coal, driven by its Carbon Price Floor, demonstrates that a combination of price signals and regulatory mandates can achieve rapid, comprehensive decarbonization. The political will to adopt these proven solutions remains the primary barrier, fueled by the 990 billion dollars in annual excess revenue that purchases institutional inaction.

agents.insider:
Insiders and whistleblowers play a critical role in exposing the hidden welfare costs and institutional failures that perpetuate coal combustion. Their actions directly challenge the Carbon Lock-In axiom by making welfare destruction partially observable from within the bilateral transaction. Several legal frameworks protect and incentivize these individuals.

In the United States, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 806 (18 U.S.C. Section 1514A) protects employees of publicly traded companies who report violations of Securities and Exchange Commission rules, including material misrepresentation of environmental liabilities. Coal companies with 2.4 billion dollars in self-bonded, unfunded reclamation liabilities, or those failing to disclose the full scope of coal ash remediation costs, stranded-asset write-down risk, or pending environmental enforcement actions, are subject to these rules. Whistleblowers reporting such issues to the Securities and Exchange Commission can receive remedies including reinstatement, back pay, and compensatory damages, with awards ranging from 250,000 dollars to over 40 million dollars.

The Clean Air Act Section 113(f) (42 U.S.C. Section 7413(f)) provides citizen-suit authority and protects informants who report violations of emission standards. If a coal plant is exceeding Mercury and Air Toxics Standards limits, operating without required Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems, or falsifying emission reports, this statute authorizes enforcement actions with penalties up to 109,024 dollars per day per violation. This mechanism helps to make the welfare costs of air pollution legally attributable.

For coal mine workers, the Mine Safety and Health Act Section 105(c) (30 U.S.C. Section 815(c)) protects those who report safety violations, including excessive silica dust exposure, which is a primary driver of progressive massive fibrosis (black lung disease). Despite over 4,000 cases of progressive massive fibrosis since 2010, the Mine Safety and Health Administration's official count was only 99, indicating a severe undercount. Miners reporting dust-monitoring manipulation are engaging in legally protected activity and can be awarded reinstatement, back pay, and compensatory damages. The Environmental Protection Agency's Environmental Justice Collaborative Problem-Solving grants also provide 30,000–120,000 dollars to community organizations documenting violations, offering another avenue for reporting and support.

Insiders possess unique access to information that can crack the opacity of corporate operations, revealing the true extent of welfare destruction and the mechanisms of its concealment. Their actions provide crucial evidence that can activate regulators, plaintiffs, and investors, thereby weakening the Carbon Lock-In and Stranded Asset Capture axioms.
agents.plaintiff:
Litigation pathways offer a powerful mechanism for internalizing the unpriced welfare costs of coal combustion, directly challenging the Carbon Lock-In axiom by imposing financial accountability. Plaintiffs, including affected communities, environmental groups, and public health organizations, can pursue various causes of action to seek damages and injunctive relief.

Common causes of action include tort claims for nuisance, trespass, and negligence, particularly for localized harms such as air pollution, water contamination from coal ash, and health impacts from mountaintop removal. For example, communities affected by coal ash pond leaks, which contaminate groundwater at 91 percent of U.S. sites, can sue for property damage, health costs, and remediation. The Duke Energy coal ash spill in 2014, which dumped 39,000 tons of ash into the Dan River, led to significant litigation and settlements.

Discovery targets in such cases often include internal company documents related to environmental compliance, health impact assessments, lobbying activities, and financial projections for environmental liabilities. These documents can reveal suppressed toxicology data, concealed environmental monitoring results, and deliberate strategies to externalize costs. Precedent cases, such as the multi-billion dollar settlements in per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances litigation and historical asbestos and tobacco lawsuits, demonstrate the potential for massive damages when long-term, widespread harms are proven. These cases create a financial signal that activates investors and regulators, forcing a re-evaluation of coal's true economic viability.

Likely damages can include compensatory damages for property value diminution, medical expenses, lost wages due to illness, and pain and suffering. Punitive damages may also be awarded in cases of gross negligence or willful misconduct. Furthermore, injunctive relief can force companies to cease harmful operations, install pollution controls, or undertake extensive remediation efforts. The threat and reality of such litigation contribute to the increasing stranded asset risk faced by coal companies, making their continued operation less attractive to investors.

Litigation also provides an evidentiary foundation for other agents. Information uncovered during discovery can be used by regulators to strengthen enforcement actions, by legislators to craft new laws, and by investors to refine their risk assessments. The ability of plaintiffs to make welfare costs legally attributable, even when not independently computable by market mechanisms, is a crucial component of the 'Conflictoring Protocol,' which aims to deploy multiple agent types simultaneously against welfare-destroying activities. This collective pressure helps to break the structural barriers that allow coal's welfare destruction to persist unpriced.
agents.regulator:
Regulators at various levels possess statutory authority and enforcement tools to address coal's welfare destruction, but their effectiveness is often hampered by political capture and jurisdictional gaps. Their primary role is to create information architecture that makes system welfare (ΔW) measurable where market pricing cannot, thereby challenging the Carbon Lock-In axiom.

In the United States, the Environmental Protection Agency has jurisdiction over air and water pollution from coal. Enforcement tools include setting National Ambient Air Quality Standards for pollutants like particulate matter 2.5 and sulfur dioxide, issuing permits under the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System for water discharges, and mandating pollution control technologies. The Mercury and Air Toxics Standards, for example, significantly reduced mercury emissions from coal plants. However, the Environmental Protection Agency's authority over greenhouse gases has been contentious, with the Supreme Court limiting its power to mandate broad emissions reductions. The Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement oversees mining practices, but self-bonding loopholes have allowed companies to avoid reclamation liabilities, leading to externalized costs.

In the European Union, the European Commission enforces the Emissions Trading System and various environmental directives. Member states' environmental agencies implement these at the national level. The European Union Emissions Trading System, while pricing carbon, has been criticized for free permit allocation and insufficient price levels to drive rapid coal phase-out. Enforcement gaps arise from national exemptions and the political economy of coal-dependent regions.

China's Ministry of Ecology and Environment enforces Ultra-Low Emission Standards, which have dramatically reduced local air pollution from coal plants. However, the focus has been on end-of-pipe controls rather than fuel switching, allowing carbon dioxide emissions to continue growing. Enforcement is centralized but faces the challenge of balancing environmental goals with energy security and economic growth.

The gaps in regulatory enforcement are significant. First, carbon pricing is either absent or too low in most major coal-consuming nations to reflect the true Social Cost of Carbon (190 dollars per tonne). Second, regulatory capture, as evidenced by the coal industry's extensive lobbying, leads to delayed or weakened standards. Third, jurisdictional arbitrage allows companies to shift operations to countries with laxer regulations. Fourth, the sheer scale of coal's externalities, particularly climate damages and transboundary air pollution, often exceeds the mandate or capacity of single national regulators.

To be effective, regulators must price at the break-even institutional efficiency (μ* = 0.164), not at political convenience. This implies a minimum effective carbon price of approximately 30–50 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide or equivalent regulatory burdens. They must also bifurcate regulatory targets, addressing both climate damage (via carbon pricing or emission performance standards) and mortality damage (via ambient air quality standards and source-specific emission limits). The cost of the fix is dwarfed by the cost of the problem, with benefit-cost ratios exceeding 300:1 for some regulations. The challenge for regulators is to overcome political friction and enforce standards that align private incentives with system welfare.
agents.legislator:
Legislators hold the ultimate power to rewrite the rules of the game, making them the only agents capable of directly breaking the Combustion Identity axiom by mandating production bans, mandatory substitution timelines, or supply caps. Their role is crucial in transforming the structural conditions that perpetuate coal's welfare destruction.

In the United States, relevant committees include the Senate Committee on Environment and Public Works, the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, and appropriations committees. Bills that exist or are needed include comprehensive carbon pricing legislation (e.g., a carbon tax or a cap-and-trade system with a robust price floor), which has been repeatedly defeated due to coal-state political influence. The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, while not directly banning coal, provides significant clean energy tax credits that make new renewables cheaper than existing coal, indirectly accelerating phase-out. However, direct legislative mandates for coal plant retirement or a national clean energy standard are still needed.

In the European Union, the European Parliament and Council legislate on climate and energy policy. The 'Fit for 55' package and the European Green Deal aim to reduce emissions, but specific coal phase-out laws are often left to member states. Germany's Kohlekommission, a legislative process leading to a 2038 coal phase-out law, is a prime example of a structural intervention. However, the timeline is too slow for a 1.5 degrees Celsius pathway.

China's National People's Congress and State Council set national energy policy. While China has aggressive renewable targets, it continues to approve new coal capacity, often for grid stability. Legislation mandating a hard cap on coal consumption or a rapid phase-out timeline, rather than just 'flexibilization,' is needed to address the Carbon Lock-In axiom directly.

The structural intervention required is a clear, legally binding phase-out schedule for non-essential coal combustion, coupled with robust just transition funding. The United Kingdom's Carbon Price Floor, a national fiscal law, effectively achieved this. Legislators must also expand liability for environmental damages, remove regulatory preemption that shields coal companies, and reform campaign finance laws to reduce the influence of industry lobbying. The 990 billion dollars in annual excess revenue from non-essential coal combustion funds political inaction, making legislative courage paramount. The annual cost of a global just transition (40–83 billion dollars) is less than 8 percent of this excess revenue, demonstrating that the fiscal barrier is negligible compared to the political one. Legislators must prioritize system welfare over the narrow interests of a declining industry, using their power to enact the structural changes that markets alone cannot deliver.
agents.investor:
Investors face significant financial exposure to the welfare costs of coal combustion, primarily through stranded asset risk and potential litigation liabilities. The System Asset Pricing Model provides critical metrics for portfolio screening and risk assessment, directly challenging the Carbon Lock-In axiom by repricing βW as stranded-asset risk.

Coal's system beta of 6.95 means that for every dollar of revenue, 6.10 dollars in system welfare are destroyed. This translates into a high probability of future regulatory action, carbon pricing, and litigation that will erode profitability. Investment in coal assets is equivalent to holding an asset with a deeply negative system-adjusted payoff (5.894 trillion dollars per year), indicating that the activity destroys more value than it creates. This makes coal a 'Hollow Win'—an industry whose apparent economic success is illusory when welfare costs are properly accounted for.

Portfolio screening criteria should prioritize companies with clear, credible coal phase-out plans and robust investments in renewable energy. Investors should divest from thermal coal holdings or engage with boards to demand accelerated retirement timelines. Stress-testing portfolios against carbon price scenarios of 50–190 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide is essential, as these price levels would render most coal assets unprofitable. Carbon Tracker's analysis shows that 42 percent of global coal capacity was already unprofitable by 2018, and 96 percent will be by 2030. This means that the vast majority of existing coal assets are at risk of premature retirement before their capital costs are fully amortized.

Companies most exposed include utilities with young coal fleets, mining companies heavily reliant on thermal coal sales, and financial institutions that provide loans or insurance to the coal sector. For example, between 2017 and 2024, approximately 50 major insurers and reinsurers adopted coal exclusion policies, leading to a 200–400 percent increase in coal insurance costs for projects in affected markets. This private-sector risk assessment, incorporating welfare costs, acts as an exogenous game transformation, making coal projects uninsurable and unfinanceable.

Investors must recognize that the economic argument for coal is over. The cheapest coal plant to retire is the one that was never built. For existing assets, the question is not whether they will be stranded, but whether the stranding is managed proactively or imposed through bankruptcy and regulatory action. Companies that delay managed transition will negotiate from a position of financial distress, as evidenced by the bankruptcies of Peabody, Alpha, and Arch, which externalized billions in liabilities. By incorporating βW into their financial models, investors can drive capital away from welfare-destroying activities and accelerate the transition to a cleaner economy.
agents.supranational:
Supranational bodies and international treaties are essential for addressing coal's global welfare destruction, particularly by preventing jurisdictional arbitrage that undermines unilateral national actions and by defining property rights in global commons. Their role is to coordinate minimum standards across jurisdictions, thereby challenging the Stranded Asset Capture axiom.

The Paris Agreement, a landmark international treaty, sets a framework for national climate commitments, but its voluntary nature and lack of binding enforcement mechanisms limit its effectiveness. While it encourages nations to submit Nationally Determined Contributions, these are often insufficient to meet the 1.5 degrees Celsius target. The Powering Past Coal Alliance, with 180 members, aims to accelerate coal phase-out but lacks the enforcement power to compel action from major coal consumers.

The South Africa Just Energy Transition Partnership, a multilateral financing pledge, demonstrates the limitations of current international frameworks. Its 96 percent loan-to-grant ratio meant that transition costs were transferred to the sovereign balance sheet rather than internalized by coal producers, leading to near-zero coal reduction. This highlights the need for grant-heavy financing in developing economies to make just transitions politically and economically feasible.

Effective international frameworks must include binding mechanisms with compliance penalties. The Montreal Protocol (1987), which achieved a near-universal phase-out of chlorofluorocarbons, serves as a precedent. Its success was due to scientific consensus, industry willingness (DuPont had substitutes), and financial mechanisms for developing nations, coupled with trade provisions that prevented competitive leakage. For coal, a similar framework would need to: (a) establish a global carbon price floor or equivalent emission performance standards, (b) create a substantial, grant-based global just transition fund, and (c) implement carbon border adjustment mechanisms to prevent carbon leakage and protect early-moving jurisdictions.

The European Union's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism is a step in this direction, imposing tariffs on carbon-intensive imports to level the playing field. International organizations like the World Bank and multilateral development banks must shift from debt-heavy financing to grant-based support for coal phase-out in emerging market and developing economies. The International Energy Agency's Net Zero by 2050 scenario provides a technically and economically validated roadmap for essential coal use, offering a common baseline for international action. Supranational bodies must define and enforce property rights over the atmospheric commons, preventing its continued use as an unpriced waste repository. This collective action is necessary to overcome the political friction and economic incentives that perpetuate coal's profound welfare destruction, which no single sovereign can address alone.

crossDomainConnections.connection: Global coal combustion (βW = 6.95) shares a critical connection with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (βW = 35.2) in the System Asset Pricing Model framework: both are classified as 'Hollow Wins,' meaning their private payoffs are structurally dependent on unpriced welfare destruction. However, their underlying impossibility structures differ significantly. For per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, the impossibility is thermodynamic and molecular: the carbon-fluorine bond's extreme strength leads to molecular persistence, bioaccumulation, and ubiquitous contamination that is literally irreversible on human timescales. The welfare cost is embedded in the chemistry of the molecule itself. For coal, the impossibility is institutional and physical: while coal's carbon intensity is a physical fact (Combustion Identity), the system-level welfare destruction is correctable through phase-out. The harm stops when combustion stops. This distinction is crucial for policy: per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances require contamination management and substitution (because the harm cannot be undone), whereas coal requires phase-out and replacement (because the harm stops). Both domains highlight the failure of markets to price harms that are externalized across time and populations, but the nature of the externality dictates different intervention strategies.

Coal combustion (βW = 6.95) and monoculture agriculture (βW = 8.6) are both classified as 'Slow Institutional Hollow Wins' in the System Asset Pricing Model taxonomy. This means their private payoffs are structurally dependent on unpriced welfare destruction, and their failures are correctable through institutional reform rather than being axiomatically impossible. Both activities generate significant private revenue (990 billion dollars for coal, 340 billion dollars for monoculture) while imposing massive, slow-accumulating welfare costs. For monoculture, these costs include biodiversity loss, soil degradation, pesticide externalities, and increased vulnerability to disease, which accumulate over decades. For coal, climate damages and long-term health impacts also accumulate slowly. Both domains face strong incumbent capture, where large-scale private profits from existing practices resist the adoption of economically viable, more welfare-positive alternatives (e.g., regenerative agriculture for monoculture, renewables for coal). The shared axioms often involve a form of 'lock-in' (carbon lock-in for coal, genetic lock-in for monoculture) and 'capture' (stranded asset capture for coal, regulatory capture by agribusiness for monoculture). The policy challenge for both is to overcome this institutional friction and implement reforms that internalize externalities and enable the transition to more sustainable, welfare-positive alternatives, even when the economic case for the alternative is clear.

Coal combustion (βW = 6.95) and the broader oil and gas sector (βW ≈ 3.5) are both fossil fuel domains with climate damage as a primary welfare channel. However, coal's higher system beta reflects three structural differences. First, coal's carbon intensity per unit of energy is approximately two times higher than natural gas and 1.3 times higher than oil, meaning each dollar of coal revenue generates more carbon dioxide per unit of useful energy. Second, coal's air pollution mortality burden is approximately five times higher than natural gas per unit of electricity generated, due to higher emissions of particulate matter 2.5, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and mercury. Third, coal's extraction process, particularly mountaintop removal, produces welfare costs that have no direct analogue in conventional oil and gas production. While both sectors face similar challenges of Carbon Lock-In and Stranded Asset Capture, coal's unique chemical composition and extraction methods lead to a more intense welfare destruction per dollar of revenue. Policy spillovers are significant: a strong carbon price for coal can create a competitive advantage for natural gas, but a comprehensive climate policy must address both, ultimately favoring zero-emission alternatives. The distinction highlights that not all fossil fuels are created equal in their welfare impact, necessitating differentiated policy approaches.

faq.q: The paper claims coal is a 'Hollow Win.' What does that mean, and how can an industry generating 1.1 trillion dollars in revenue be 'hollow'?
faq.a: The term 'Hollow Win' signifies that the private payoff generated by an activity is structurally dependent on the welfare destruction it causes. For global coal combustion, the industry generates 1.1 trillion dollars in total annual revenue, with 990 billion dollars of that classified as 'excess private payoff' beyond essential uses. However, the system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA), which subtracts the annual welfare destruction (6.884 trillion dollars) from the total private payoff, is a deeply negative 5.894 trillion dollars per year. This means the industry destroys far more value than it creates. The 'hollowness' arises because this 990 billion dollars in excess revenue does not exist because coal provides value that renewables cannot; it exists precisely because the massive externalities—climate damages, air pollution mortality, mercury neurotoxicity—are not priced into the market. If the carbon dioxide externality alone were priced at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's 190 dollars per tonne, coal-fired power would be instantaneously uneconomic in virtually every market. The private payoff is 'hollow' in the sense that it depends for its existence on the continued non-pricing of the welfare destruction it causes. Remove the atmospheric subsidy, and the revenue evaporates. The industry appears economically successful by conventional metrics (revenue, employment, tax revenue), but this success is an illusion, a statistical artifact of excluding 6.884 trillion dollars in welfare costs from its accounting. The United Kingdom's experience, where a carbon price floor of 18 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide led to a complete coal phase-out, empirically demonstrates how quickly this 'hollow' revenue collapses when externalities are internalized. The industry's apparent profitability is a direct measure of society's failure to price its true costs.

faq.q: The paper uses a system beta (βW) of 6.95. Is this just another way of saying coal is bad, or does it offer a new insight?
faq.a: The system beta (βW) of 6.95 is not merely a qualitative statement that coal is 'bad'; it is a precise, dimensionless ratio that offers a new, comparable insight into the structural relationship between private payoff and system welfare destruction. Analogous to the market beta in financial economics, βW measures how much each dollar of revenue co-moves with system welfare destruction. A βW of 6.95 means that for every dollar of excess private payoff generated by non-essential coal combustion, 6.10 dollars in system welfare are destroyed. This ratio is robustly calculated through 100,000 Monte Carlo simulations, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [5.7, 8.5], and a 0.0000 percent probability that βW is less than 1 (i.e., welfare-positive). This quantitative metric allows for direct, apples-to-apples comparison across diverse industrial sectors and policy domains. For instance, it reveals that coal's per-dollar welfare cost exceeds nuclear power (βW = 0.7) by a factor of 8.7. This is not a subjective judgment but a calibrated number derived from data published by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, the World Health Organization, and the International Energy Agency. The insight is that βW provides a unified pricing framework for externalities, enabling policymakers and investors to make direct welfare-cost comparisons across environmental and technological risk categories on a common analytical footing. It transforms the diffuse concept of 'externalities' into a concrete, actionable risk metric, revealing the true cost of private economic activity to the broader system.

faq.q: The paper claims an 'Impossibility Theorem' for coal. Does this mean we can't do anything about coal's harms?
faq.a:
The Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem does not imply inaction; rather, it dictates the *type* of action required. It proves that no market mechanism, Pigouvian tax set at politically feasible levels, or voluntary industry reform can reduce coal's system beta (βW) below 3.9 through private action alone. This 'impossibility floor' means the welfare gap is structurally irreducible from *within* the existing market and institutional architecture. The theorem rests on three axioms: Combustion Identity (coal inherently produces carbon dioxide), Carbon Lock-In (existing infrastructure prevents rational early exit), and Stranded Asset Capture (industry political power blocks corrective policies). As long as these three axioms hold, the market cannot solve the problem.

However, the theorem explicitly states that exogenous institutional restructuring—changes to the rules of the game imposed from *outside* the game—can reduce βW below this floor by violating one or more axioms. For example, the United Kingdom's Carbon Price Floor, a national fiscal intervention, successfully achieved a complete coal phase-out, demonstrating that the welfare failure is a matter of institutional capacity and political will, not a mathematical inevitability. Effective interventions must target at least one axiom: developing alternative production processes (targeting Combustion Identity), mandating disclosure of welfare costs (targeting Carbon Lock-In), or breaking institutional barriers to self-correction through campaign finance reform and independent regulatory bodies (targeting Stranded Asset Capture). The impossibility theorem is a precise diagnosis, not a death sentence. It clarifies that the market cannot fix itself and that deliberate, structural policy interventions are not just desirable but fundamentally necessary to overcome coal's embedded welfare destruction.

methodology:
The System Asset Pricing Model (SAPM) calibration for global coal combustion is a rigorous, multi-disciplinary analysis integrating financial economics, welfare economics, environmental epidemiology, and energy systems modeling. The methodology is designed to produce a single, comparable, dimensionless ratio—the system beta (βW)—that quantifies the welfare destruction per dollar of revenue.

**Data Sources:** The paper draws on a comprehensive array of authoritative data sources. Global coal demand (8.8 billion tonnes) and carbon dioxide emissions (15 gigatonnes) are sourced from the International Energy Agency's 'Coal 2025' report. Market revenue estimates (1.1 trillion dollars) are from Fortune Business Insights. The cooperative baseline of 880 million tonnes of essential-use coal is derived from the International Energy Agency's Net Zero by 2050 scenario, validated by bottom-up analysis of metallurgical, chemical feedstock, and residual industrial requirements. Welfare cost channels are calibrated using: the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's 2023 Social Cost of Carbon (190 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide) for climate damages; World Health Organization's 2024 global average Value of Statistical Life (3.76 million dollars) and Lelieveld et al. (2023) mortality estimates for air pollution; International Energy Agency's Global Methane Tracker for coal mine methane; Hendryx et al. (2009) for mountaintop removal harms; Trasande et al. (2005) for mercury neurotoxicity; and OpenSecrets, International Energy Agency subsidy tracking, and bankruptcy records for governance failure. Every dollar figure is traceable to published government or academic sources.

**Calibration Methodology:** The core of the calibration involves defining a cooperative baseline (W0) and identifying six distinct welfare-cost channels. The cooperative baseline represents the system welfare that would obtain if coal production were restricted to essential uses with no viable substitute, consistent with a 1.5 degrees Celsius climate pathway. The six channels—climate damages, air pollution mortality, coal mine methane, extraction harms, mercury neurotoxicity, and governance failure—are causally distinct, tracing non-overlapping physical or institutional pathways from coal activity to welfare destruction. Each channel's annual welfare cost (ΔWk) is quantified using dose-response coefficients (e.g., Social Cost of Carbon, Value of Statistical Life) multiplied by the relevant physical quantity (e.g., tonnes of carbon dioxide, number of deaths). The total welfare destruction (ΔW) is the sum of these channel costs. The excess revenue denominator (Π) is calculated as the total market revenue minus the cooperative-baseline private payoff. The system beta (βW) is then computed as ΔW divided by Π.

**Monte Carlo Simulation:** To quantify parametric uncertainty, a Monte Carlo simulation of 100,000 draws is performed. Each channel's welfare cost is modeled as a lognormal random variable, bounded by empirically derived uncertainty ranges. A uniform correlation coefficient (ρ = 0.3) is applied across all channel pairs via a Cholesky decomposition. The simulation aggregates these draws to produce a distribution of βW, from which the median, 90 percent confidence interval, and probability of βW < 1 are derived. This robust approach ensures that the findings are not sensitive to specific point estimates but reflect the full range of plausible outcomes.

**Limitations Acknowledged by the Author:** The paper explicitly acknowledges several limitations. The Value of Statistical Life, a major component of the air pollution mortality channel, is a contested parameter, and its global average obscures regional variations. The Social Cost of Carbon is a modeled estimate, vulnerable to discount rate assumptions and political manipulation. The central mortality estimate of 1 million deaths per year is conservative, with upper-bound estimates suggesting 2.5 million. The governance channel carries the widest uncertainty due to the difficulty of monetizing lobbying and regulatory capture. The cooperative baseline is a simplification, and a more granular analysis might assign non-zero excess welfare cost to essential-use coal. The analysis is static, a snapshot of 2024–2025, rather than a dynamic model of transition. Finally, environmental justice costs are not fully monetized, as the framework treats all deaths equally without assigning additional weight to those resulting from distributive injustice.

**What Would Change the Result:** The SAPM classification of coal as an Atmospheric Hollow Win with βW = 6.95 would be falsified by: carbon capture achieving less than 50 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide at greater than 90 percent capture rate at scale; air pollution mortality from coal being demonstrated to be below 200,000 deaths per year globally; the Social Cost of Carbon being demonstrated to be below 16 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide; global institutional recapture (μ) exceeding the break-even threshold of 0.164; or renewable plus storage costs *increasing* rather than decreasing, restoring coal's economic viability. The paper demonstrates that even under the most conservative assumptions on every dimension, βW remains greater than 1, confirming the robustness of the conclusion that coal combustion is system-welfare-destroying.

timeline.significance: Coal powered the Industrial Revolution with no accounting for externalities. The atmosphere was treated as an infinite waste repository, and welfare costs per tonne were lower due to less cumulative atmospheric carbon dioxide.
Over 4,000 deaths in four days catalyzed the United Kingdom Clean Air Act 1956, marking the beginning of local harm recognition for coal pollution.
Established ambient air quality standards in the United States, further recognizing and partially addressing local externalities of coal combustion.
Achieved near-universal phase-out of chlorofluorocarbons, demonstrating that international treaties can address global environmental problems, though coal presents different challenges.

timeline.event: IPCC First Assessment Report & U.S. Acid Rain Program
timeline.significance: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change report initiated the climate awakening, recognizing carbon dioxide as a dominant coal externality. The Acid Rain Program demonstrated market-based mechanisms could reduce sulfur dioxide, but global carbon dioxide was largely ignored.

timeline.event: American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity Lobbying
timeline.significance: The American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity spent 126.4 million dollars on 'clean coal' messaging, delaying effective climate legislation and illustrating the governance failure channel.

timeline.event: Epstein et al. 'Full Cost Accounting for the Life Cycle of Coal' Published
timeline.significance: This Harvard Medical School study provided a comprehensive single-source estimate of coal externalities, informing the multi-channel architecture of the System Asset Pricing Model.
Established the stranded-asset framework, highlighting the macro-financial risks of premature coal retirement and contributing to the Carbon Lock-In axiom.

timeline.event: United Kingdom Carbon Price Floor Implemented
timeline.significance: Set at 16 pounds per tonne of carbon dioxide, this national tax made coal-fired generation uneconomic, leading to a rapid phase-out and demonstrating institutional correction feasibility.
Mandated significant reductions in local air pollutants from coal plants, improving public health but not addressing carbon dioxide emissions, illustrating channel-specific effectiveness.
New solar and wind became cheaper than new coal plants globally, marking the first of four crossover thresholds for coal's economic viability.
Led to a legislated coal phase-out by 2038, backed by 40 billion euros in compensation, serving as a global benchmark for just transition design.
Temporarily increased coal use due to supply disruptions, but ultimately accelerated the long-term phase-out trajectory rather than reversing it.
New solar plus storage became cheaper than the marginal operating cost of existing coal plants in major markets, marking the second crossover threshold.

timeline.event: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's Social Cost of Carbon Update
timeline.significance: Finalized at 190 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide, nearly quadrupling the Obama-era estimate and providing a critical input for monetizing climate damages.

timeline.event: Lelieveld et al. Mortality Estimates Published
timeline.significance: Updated global estimates of fossil fuel-attributable deaths, providing a key data source for the air pollution mortality channel, with conservative estimates of 1 million coal-specific deaths per year.

timeline.event: United Kingdom Achieves Zero Coal Power
timeline.significance: The United Kingdom became the first major economy to completely exit coal power, demonstrating the feasibility of institutional correction within a decade.

The International Energy Agency's 'Coal 2025' report identifies this period as the peak of global coal demand, with production plateauing before entering structural decline, driven by Chinese and Indian generation declines.
For the first time outside an economic contraction, China's coal-fired power output fell by 1.6 percent, signaling a structural shift driven by massive renewable energy deployment.

literatureContext:
This paper, 'Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Global Coal Combustion,' positions itself at the intersection of five converging disciplinary literatures, extending prior results and offering a novel analytical framework for understanding the system welfare costs of industrial activities. It builds upon and contributes to climate economics, environmental epidemiology, energy transition economics, environmental justice, and political economy.

**Climate Economics and the Social Cost of Carbon:** The paper directly integrates the foundational work in climate economics, particularly the concept of the Social Cost of Carbon. Originating with Nordhaus (1992) and evolving through the Stern Review (2006) and Pindyck's (2013) critiques of Integrated Assessment Models, this literature provides the monetization framework for carbon dioxide-driven climate damages. The paper utilizes the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency's 2023 updated Social Cost of Carbon of 190 dollars per tonne of carbon dioxide, which incorporates updated damage functions from Rennert et al. (2022) in Nature. This represents a significant increase from previous estimates, driven by a more accurate capture of mortality from temperature extremes. The System Asset Pricing Model absorbs this output as the dose-response coefficient for its climate-damage channel, but critically, it sidesteps the contentious discount rate debate by operating as a contemporaneous ratio (βW), measuring current-year welfare destruction against current-year private payoff.

**Environmental Epidemiology and the Value of Statistical Life:** The paper draws heavily on environmental epidemiology to quantify the human health costs of coal. Building on Pope et al.'s (2002) work on particulate matter 2.5 and cardiovascular mortality, it incorporates recent updates from Lelieveld et al. (2023) in The BMJ, which estimated 5.13 million annual deaths from fossil fuel air pollution. Henneman et al. (2023) in Science further demonstrated that coal-derived particulate matter 2.5 is 2.1 times more lethal than particulate matter 2.5 from other sources. The monetization bridge from deaths to dollars is provided by the Value of Statistical Life literature, anchored by Viscusi and Aldy (2003) and updated by the World Health Organization's 2024 global estimate of 3.76 million dollars. The System Asset Pricing Model integrates these mortality estimates into its air pollution mortality channel, offering a comprehensive, global perspective that extends beyond single-jurisdiction health impact assessments.

**Energy Transition Economics and Stranded Assets:** The paper extends the stranded asset framework, pioneered by Carbon Tracker's 'Unburnable Carbon' report (2012). It incorporates findings that 42 percent of global coal capacity was unprofitable by 2018, with projections of 96 percent by 2030. Work by Diluiso et al. (2021) and Cui et al. (2025) on managed phase-out pathways informs the paper's understanding of transition dynamics. The paper's governance-failure channel explicitly quantifies the costs of stranded-asset socialization and the financial risks of premature coal retirement, filling a gap in standard externality accounting. The cancellation of projects like Cirebon-1 in Indonesia highlights the ongoing challenges in financing orderly transitions, which the System Asset Pricing Model framework helps to contextualize.

**Environmental Justice and Energy Equity:** The analysis is deeply informed by the environmental justice literature, starting with Bullard's (1990) 'Dumping in Dixie.' It documents the disproportionate burden of coal's harms on low-income and minority communities, citing the NAACP's 'Coal Blooded' report that 78 percent of African Americans live within 30 miles of a coal plant. Carley and Konisky's (2025) work on the distributional inequity of decarbonization benefits is also relevant. The System Asset Pricing Model's recognition-justice condition directly maps to the Private Pareto Theorem's axioms on welfare distribution, providing a quantitative overlay to qualitative justice concerns. Hendryx et al.'s work on mountaintop removal health costs further illustrates the localized and inequitable welfare drains.

**Political Economy and Regulatory Capture:** The paper integrates insights from the political economy literature, particularly Stigler's (1971) theory of regulatory capture. It provides empirical evidence of the coal sector's lobbying expenditures (e.g., 126.4 million dollars by the American Coalition for Clean Coal Electricity) and its influence on policy, such as Peabody Energy's funding of climate-denial organizations. This strand illuminates why institutional recapture (μ) systematically falls short of the break-even threshold (μ*), which is a core finding of the paper. The System Asset Pricing Model's governance-failure channel explicitly monetizes these costs, demonstrating that regulatory capture is not merely context but a load-bearing channel of welfare destruction.

**Difference from Conventional Analysis:** This paper differs from conventional analysis by providing a single, comparable, dimensionless welfare-cost metric (βW) that integrates diverse externalities into a unified pricing framework. Unlike Integrated Assessment Models, which are sensitive to discount rates and often omit tipping-point risks, the System Asset Pricing Model operates as a contemporaneous ratio, sidestepping the discount rate debate. It moves beyond piecemeal externality accounting to offer a holistic 'system-level' perspective, enabling direct cross-domain comparisons. The paper's formalization of the Carbon Intensity Floor Impossibility Theorem further distinguishes it by proving that coal's welfare gap is structurally irreducible through market mechanisms alone, necessitating exogenous institutional restructuring. This theoretical contribution, combined with its comprehensive empirical calibration, positions the paper as a foundational work in structural welfare economics.
